teachings.teaching: Kripa (causeless mercy) is the operative ingredient of the spiritual path — neither hope nor effort but grace plus luck plus Time — and can be granted to a yogin who is cursing rather than praying.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's first parikrama earned him the Hill's mercy precisely while he was 'almost naked, frozen, and swearing'. Grace is unsentimental.

teachings.teaching: Heightened empathy is a yogic side-effect that absorbs others' suffering into the yogin's own body — a real transfer, not a metaphor; the yogin must learn to feel without absorbing.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's blisters were the elderly woman's blisters; this is the cost of unguarded compassion and an instruction in controlled redistribution of life-energy.

teachings.teaching: Faith — not hope — is the precondition for yogic success; hope is a weed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's pedagogical line: 'Not hope, but faith… well, that and grace, and luck, and Time.'

teachings.teaching: After samadhi the body becomes audible from within — heart as thunder, neurons as fireworks, intestines as artillery, Nadis as power-lines; the mind, not the ears, hears it.
teachings.lens_note: The inner symphony as a stable side-effect of stabilised samadhi — anatomy as cosmos.

results.english:
...in darkness, and the heart was a chamber where condensed, perfect darkness was held, slowly pumping invisible blood through itself. In the heart an ancient power was hidden, and it kept the engine of life in unceasing motion. Amid all this darkness there was also light, entering the body through the eyes, mouth, and ears; it was at once swallowed by the inner dark.

Numerous lights flashed in the brain, and this hidden power-station sent electrical discharges throughout the whole body, charging the nervous system. Once, "observing" this epic scene on the verge of morning, he discovered a sort of "sunrise" right beneath the skin. The body began to absorb the diffuse shimmer of dawn, and this dawn was beautiful: rays of light broke through between the fingers of the hands and feet, where the skin is thinner, and all of it stimulated electrical discharges, accelerating the movement of neurons. He also became aware of the presence of millions of living beings: among the red and white blood cells moved viruses and bacteria, lawful inhabitants of the Universe of the body; to some extent the consciousness of these beings affected the mind of the "host."

The awareness of the vast variety of forms of life allowed him to focus on a new, hitherto unexplored aspect of the body's life. As soon as attention was fixed on the microbes, consciousness began to advance deeper, examining, as under a microscope, ever smaller forms of life that lived upon the microbes themselves. Now protons, no longer atoms, became "visible," and this investigation of the structure of the building blocks of the inner Universe enabled him to grasp the colossal scale of the body in comparison with the forms of life that inhabited it. For consciousness to expand, the mind must come to the "source of its own origin," to a sharp sense of selfhood. Witnessing the immensity of the body, surrounded by inconceivable galaxies of the greater world, the mind could overcome its restriction to any form and become truly infinite.

When he came out of trance, the heartbeat was restored, the blood-flow resumed its triumphant surging current, and its sound was hypnotic. The crackling of the brain's neurons was accompanied by the rolls of drum-beats and the sounds of the percussion instruments of the orchestra of the intestines. The reverberations of the cracking of electromagnetic discharges throughout the body formed the general background of this mighty symphony.

All these sounds were different notes of the Void. Transcendental reality can completely distract the one who lives through this experience, wrapping him in itself. Attempts to share such experiences with others can be mistakenly taken for hallucinations or psychoses, but mystical experiences always have a personal transformative effect. He was far from labelling all these experiences. It is enough to be a reverent observer, a willing participant. Calm acceptance was the key to the continuation of the show. He no longer stood at the entrance of the theatre; he had a ticket in his own private box.

...Almost twenty-five years later someone gave him an album of the artist Alex Grey, who had expressed the symbolism of the New Age in the language of psychedelic realism and had created an anatomical atlas of cosmic consciousness. The transparent human figures were as though run through a wild colourful X-ray machine. Every bone, organ and vein was detailed in vivid colours, objects and spaces were connected by a dense web of neural threads, and the organs were lit from within by electricity. In some pictures the basic human functions — birth, sex, and death — were depicted abstractly, even lifelessly. This hallucinogenic art could easily be classified as fantasy, but it was extremely close to what he had "seen" during his expeditions into the depths of his own shell. Drawn by Cosmic consciousness, yogic self-identification was not bounded by the body; the awareness of one's own "I" became commensurate with the world in its broader sense.

To perceive the body as a Universe was astonishing, especially after the limits of that very body had been transcended. He knew he was outside, and yet the awareness of the body's splendour was a necessary education, a boon. This mystical journey gave him the sense of inner and outer unity with the wholeness of the world. Ordinary time and space were transcended, replaced by infinity and eternity, and this experience was beyond all concepts and words.

Once unveiled, the final nature of being was more real than the phenomenal world experienced in ordinary states of consciousness. Awaking from sleep a man usually enters the "reality" of waking and dismisses the "unreality" of the dream. The full awakening to the higher reality in the state of samadhi was sobering, and the dream-like character of so-called "normal" waking consciousness began to seem coarse, almost grotesque.

Reduced to verbal descriptions, these experiences sounded paradoxical: "Form is Void, and Void is formless form," "I am That." Yet both statements were true, since they described the nature of non-duality, accurately defining the state in which he now permanently dwelt.

Commentaries on the Gita

The higher pranayamas which Babaji had taught him required diligent practice. Now, possessing the technique, he could advance, and he tripled his efforts. The sadhana brought breath-taking discoveries. The awareness of the nature of Matter that composes the surrounding material world came together with the understanding that the Void which had given birth to all this Matter, including his own body, was conscious. This new understanding went hand in hand with the realisation of the vastness and incredible complexity of life and the great logic of its cycles. The innocence and simplicity of Nature, manifested even in her most "cruel" aspects, drove him mad and pacified him at the same time. He felt a strong need to discuss these discoveries; disciples and friends listened to his monologues with bated breath. Yet the themes were too grand for purely intellectual comprehension; only those who had already journeyed beyond their own bodies could appreciate them — and there were no such interlocutors in his immediate circle.

This absorbed and assimilated knowledge reached its apogee at Govardhan, during the spontaneous parikrama. The flow of thoughts quickened, and now he somehow understood and "saw" the vast structure of the interrelation of the primary elements that gave rise to Matter, and everything in this visible material world lived according to laws issuing from the very nature of those elements. The mastery of the astral planes could wait; first he had to understand gross reality.

Returning with relief to the farm, to the serenity of the cottage, he sensed a feeling of invisible presence, as though some great force had followed him from the parikrama itself and now took up residence near him. The intellect acquired depth, becoming more analytical. But this depth was not a property of the logical mind; it was a subconscious certainty in the truth that lay at the core of any question. Whenever he replied to a question, his angle on the problem was unexpected, and the style struck listeners with the precision of definition and simplicity. The analysis led to a practical solution that always worked. He had no idea how.

...Once, when he was visiting his aunt in Mathura, his younger cousin Devahuti, interested in spiritualism, proposed a séance. Together with another friend, the three of them sat down on the floor and began the ritual. All three of them simultaneously felt that some force or entity had entered the room, and Devahuti asked for confirmation of the spirit's presence: "We would like to see you; please give a sign of your presence." The cotton curtains stirred as if charged with electricity; they received a mental message: "Close your eyes" — and saw the same thing: a grey-bearded man, resembling a siddha, sat under a large tree. Addressing Devahuti, he said: "Tell your brother — the time has come to begin the commentaries on the Bhagavad Gita." Strangely enough, this very idea had already been visiting him.

Therefore, when one of the disciples enquired about the meaning of a shloka from the Gita, he answered instantly, as though he had been waiting for the question. It was the 29th shloka of the first chapter, where Arjuna's fear before the battle is spoken of. The first reply seemed to open the inner dam holding back all the thoughts, images, and words. After he had finished his explanation, the disciple inquired: "This commentary is beautiful and concise — what book is this? I would like to read it." Evidently, the answer had sounded like a quotation from a published text. All the thoughts of recent months and years began to fall into line, to be structured, finding their connection with the chapters of the sacred book. Beginning from the 14th of January (which coincided with the day of the Sun's entry into the sign of Capricorn, the festival of Makara Sankranti), he dictated commentaries to the Bhagavad Gita daily. In those days the disciples who took dictation saw a variety of spirits, but the transparent entities, looking like plumes of light-grey smoke, never entered the territory and usually gathered beyond the gate. Mostly they were dark spirits, representatives of demonic realms. It seemed that something or someone wanted to obstruct this work, creating all manner of hindrances: sudden whirlwinds rose; lightning struck out of nowhere; the disciples taking the dictation had sudden nosebleeds; accidents of varying severity occurred; unexplainable indispositions and other interferences happened. Sometimes the disciples' relatives tried to prevent their visits to the Guru with such persistence that one could not help suspecting a stage-managed performance. Yet however irritating all these petty unpleasantnesses, they did not affect the work on the Gita.

Nature herself gave favourable signs that supported the work: like rain, luminous points scattered from the sky before sunset; in the north something resembling the aurora borealis appeared, as though the sun were rising there in spite of the approach of night. The two blue-throated jays — the nilkanthi [footnote 129: Nilkanth — blue-throated jay, Bengal roller; literally 'blue throat'; one of the names of Lord Shiva. It is said that Shiva drank the poison of the entire world, the Halahala, and, without swallowing it, kept it in his throat. From the poison the neck of Mahadev turned blue.] — daily perched on the poles behind his chair, and he continued to dictate for hours. The birds listened attentively, as though weighing every word he uttered. On completing the third chapter, a golden shadow appeared to his left, resembling a cluster of luminous energy, as though a double stood behind him. The shadow remained visible until the very end of the work on the book. Sometimes, when the flow of thoughts was especially intense, the silhouette densified and became almost opaque. Rubbing their eyes, unable to shake off the apparition, visitors carefully commented on this phenomenon, by no means sure that it was not a hallucination.

results.english:
...it was not a hallucination.

Feeling the ever-growing inner force, he exercised daily simply to subdue and discharge this energy. In addition to regular weight-lifting, he lifted disciples over his head, holding them on outstretched arms, and after such diversions the dictation went more smoothly.

According to the traditions of his line, a chapter of the Bhagavad Gita must be read every day — it is part of yogic sadhana. There existed many commentaries written by respected scholars and even saints (some were practising yogis). But not one of these authors mentioned the principal Deity, even though the sacred book called Him by name: Time. He was the supreme truth, and now He spoke to him directly. The message was striking.

He dictated words about the unbearable splendour of the union of life and death, about death as the greatest aim of life, about life as a means of knowing one's own spirit. The themes raised were grand, the immensity of the truth demanded verbal embodiment, and his words, like liberated birds, found the skies of human minds.

...The Matter of which the body is built arose from Time. From the same Time came the desire to know this body, to discover the origin of the spirit which made it alive. Spirit was the principal alchemical component, the Primordial Creator, a part of the all-generating Time. The grasp of the fleeting and transient nature of the world is possible only by knowing the various types of time and the limits within which It is contained. Everything around was placed in various vessels and countless forms, and these bottle-bodies with encoded soul-messages floated in the ocean of the Primordial Cosmos, in the Void. Each had the instrumentation to know the Creator; every body was a counter of time. Yoga, and nothing else, was the method of knowing Creation.

The Yogic Commentaries to the Bhagavad Gita were written in twenty-one days and completed on the eve of Vasant Panchami, the festival marking the arrival of spring.

The Yogis' March

While dictating, he was extremely focused and self-contained, allowing no other thoughts to intrude on the course of the exposition. Yet between sessions a vague idea continued to visit him, which soon turned into resolve. On the great Night of Shiva he was to walk from the temple of Kali to the shrine of Gupteshwar Mahadev. This thought seized him completely. He discussed the plan with the disciples, and they fully supported it. The idea of organising such a procession was not only to honour Lord Shiva; it was to remind people of the value of spiritual devotion, sadhana, and discipline — of yoga. The town lived its everyday life; for most of the inhabitants religious holidays were merely an occasion to dress up, eat well, and enjoy kirtans, and this was understandable. For many, life was a duty, an overwhelming task, and the tender shoots of spiritual search had little chance of surviving on that trampled ring where the daily battle with everyday life was fought.

Not that the procession was meant to bring about a spiritual or religious awakening of the town's people, no. To hope for that would have been too utopian. But the march of yogis could become a statement that even amid worldly life there is a place for spiritual search. He, his friends, and his disciples were part of society, and their example could illustrate the very possibility of such a striving, inspire the ordinary citizens to become heroes of their own stories. The subconscious continually reminded people of the unity of the earthly and the divine, but bustling reality demonstrated the opposite. The pragmatic approach to the very notion of spiritual awareness pointed to the widening gap between the practical aspects of life and the longing for self- and God-knowledge. Of course it had always been so, but conversations about spirituality were becoming more and more like convenient camouflage, while sincere and true aspirations were ridiculed. His own experience at the ashram had been so painful that he wanted to remind people of the unity of the material and the elevated, to demonstrate that real yoga is alive and that yogis still live among householders.

Anyway, all wished to take part in the march, although some hesitated over the choice of dress. It was assumed that, being yogis, all should put on dhotis. For places of pilgrimage this would be quite normal, but in the city, where everyone knew everyone, an appearance in a piece of cloth wrapped around the hips would cause a sensation and might be perceived as a challenge. In effect, the whole undertaking was a challenge.

On the morning of the day of Shivaratri about forty disciples and friends gathered at the cottage. Like trained bodyguards, the two blue jays again perched on the tops of the poles on either side of the path, seeing off the participants of the march. Several cars carried them to Kali Mata Mandir, the temple of Kali on the hilltop, from which they began the nineteen-kilometre march. Gwalior, jammed with festival-eve traffic and crowds, was preparing for the holiday. They walked quickly, and the mood was euphoric. This was their first "public appearance." At the sight of the procession the townspeople froze, craning their necks to get a better look at the broad-shouldered young man of powerful build with a red bandanna on his head leading the bare-chested men in dhotis. The police had to stop the traffic to clear a passage for their group through the crowd. Halting at his old house, where his relatives still lived, they refreshed themselves with drinks offered to them and continued on their way. Along the road, disciples who had not joined the procession came out into the streets, briefly stopping the march and hanging flower garlands around his neck. At one point he asked his companions to chant "Har Har Mahadev!", as he scarcely felt his own body, experiencing an unusual lightness and being in a half-trance state. Something "grounding" was needed.

Reaching the hill, they began ascending the hundred steps that led to the temple of Shiva. Mistaking them for fierce naked ascetics — naga-babas — the priest fled, exclaiming: "The Nagas are coming!" Probably he thought that the bare-chested men were going to seize the temple. The temple was now entirely at their disposal. Hundreds of people gathered for the night celebration watched their arrival with surprise and curiosity. He was overflowing with a feeling of rapture, experienced a slight giddiness as of intoxication, although he had never in his life tasted alcohol. The disciples brought milk in an iron pitcher, and he began his first Abhishek [footnote 130: Abhishek — ceremonial bathing in sacred waters; part of diksha in some traditions. The ritual symbolises the bathing of the deity to whom worship is offered. A religious method of offering prayers in which the devotee pours an offering (water, milk, sometimes even liquor) over the image of a God or Goddess.] in his life. The white liquid poured over the black stone, Shiva drank the juice of the earth itself, and the ritual was hypnotic, enveloping, suffusing him with euphoria.

In the years that followed this event he would conduct thousands of such rituals, but who could read the future then? He had no specially prepared mantra besides "Om Namah Shivaya" [footnote 131: Om Namah Shivaya — one of the most popular mantras of Hinduism, the most important mantra of Shaivism. "Namah Shivaya" means "Oh, Glory to the Almighty!" or "adoration to Lord Shiva."] — and he uttered it. Finishing the rite, he stepped slowly out of the temple and stood on the steps of the staircase leading down to gather his thoughts. A great crowd had gathered around; hundreds and thousands of people began spontaneously prostrating before him, trying to touch his feet. Charged by the force of the ritual, he hardly noticed the outstretched hands, the cries of "Har Har Mahadev!", and the heads beating against his bare feet. Forming a dense cordon, the disciples escorted him to the car, and only on returning to the farm did he notice that the big toes were bloody and the nails were gone. Too many people had beaten their heads against them, worshipping the power he was radiating. From then on he tried to avoid crowds at any cost; collective manifestations of faith and devotion are uncontrollable and burst forth like a forest fire, and he had not the slightest desire to become a mummy charred by the flame of blind belief. Instead of trying to speak with the Divine directly, people kept seeking translators for the transmission of messages from the subtle planes. He did not object to this role, but he wanted to keep for himself the right to choose the place, time, and audience. After all, even the gods are choosy in selecting their company, and he saw no reason not to follow the same strategy.

Time and Void

The samadhi that had become a part of his nightly "routine" was anything but routine. He had learned to enter the Void and then return to the capsule of the body. The trick lay in trying without trying, in passing into non-being while not regarding the passage as an attainment — so that the mind would not be smothered by bliss and awe, while gazing at the radiant infinity of the Absolute in the darkness of the majestic abyss called "the Void." The new Kriya techniques accelerated progress and, coupled with non-anticipation, were the magic key that opened the portal into Nothing which was Everything. Time ceased to be a concept; it became a living force pulsing in the Void that surrounded his true "I." It was unclear what was left of the logical mind; perhaps some fragments and particles of everyday impressions and memories. The Ancient Spirit led him through new and uncharted spaces, and this journey gave him sharp awareness of the nature of Matter, Time, and Void. But he had not the slightest idea how to clothe these experiences in verbal form.

...By the rules of the parampara, during initiation every sadhak gives a promise to read the Bhagavad Gita daily, and for several years he had faithfully kept this vow. The reading of the Gita and the practice of yoga stimulated the development of consciousness, and after some time the depth and multidimensionality of the text began to disclose hidden meanings. To bear the grandeur of the new Knowledge was not easy; it had to find an outlet, to take the form of words. The Gita became the magical catalyst that directed the flow of his reasonings. At the start of work on the commentaries he had not foreseen the scale and ambition of the project, but the dictation swept the mind down the highway of Knowledge at break-neck speed. The journey was accompanied by flashes of revelation, and the lines of the sacred text were the lane-markings, the signal-lights.

Although the Gita was a book about Time, mentions of It were not particularly many. The concept and philosophical significance of Time was timidly, in passing, alluded to in a few texts; the Veda Vyasa [footnote 132: Veda Vyasa (Krishna Dvaipayana, known as Vyasa or 'the compiler of the Vedas') is a central figure in Hindu traditions. He is considered the author of the Mahabharata, the compiler of many scriptures, and the one who divided the Vedic mantras into the four Vedas. Vyasa is also considered the author of the eighteen Puranas and the Brahma Sutras. He is one of the seven Immortals.] and the Atharva [footnote 133: Atharva Veda, Atharvaveda (from atharvanas and veda, meaning 'knowledge') is the 'bank of knowledge' of the atharvans, or 'procedures of everyday life'. This text is the fourth Veda, a late addition to the Vedic scriptures. The language of the Atharvaveda differs from Vedic Sanskrit, preserving pre-Vedic Indo-European archaisms. 730 hymns and around 6000 mantras are divided into 20 books. The Atharvaveda is sometimes called the 'Veda of magical formulae', but most scholars consider this description incorrect. It is said to represent a 'folk religion', encompassing not only the formulae of magic but also the daily rituals of initiation into learning (upanayana), weddings and funerals, and royal rituals and duties of court priests.] Veda spoke of It in brief and indefinite terms...

life_events.event: Completes the Yogic Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita in 21 days, ending on the eve of Vasant Panchami.
On Shivaratri leads a 19-km Yogis' March from Kali Mata Mandir to Gupteshwar Mahadev with ~40 disciples and friends, bare-chested in dhotis.

At Gupteshwar Mahadev the priest mistakes the group for naga-babas and flees shouting 'The Nagas are coming!'; Sharma performs his first Abhishek with milk over the black Shivalingam, chanting only Om Namah Shivaya.
Crowd of hundreds-to-thousands spontaneously prostrates and beats heads against his bare feet — his big-toenails are torn off; thereafter he resolves to avoid public crowds.
Direct cognition of Time as the supreme Deity of the Gita opens; Time is identified as Krishna's true self.

teachings.teaching: The Bhagavad Gita is a book about Time, and Krishna's 'I am Time' is the central revelation that every prior commentator missed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's signature reading: the supreme Deity of the Gita is Time itself, not Krishna-the-person; Krishna is the vessel through which Time speaks.

teachings.teaching: Matter arises from Time; Spirit is the primordial alchemical component, part of all-generating Time; every body is a counter of time, and yoga is the only method of knowing Creation.
teachings.lens_note: The metaphysics of the commentary: Matter ← Time, the body as bottle floating in the ocean of the Primordial Cosmos.

teachings.teaching: Collective manifestations of faith and devotion are uncontrollable and burst forth like a forest fire — the yogin must keep the right to choose place, time, and audience.

teachings.teaching: Real yoga is alive and yogis still live among householders; the Yogis' March was a statement, not a religious revival.

results.english:
...Apart from these stray and rather sparse fragments, discussion of Time was absent in the well-known philosophical or religious texts. In the Bhagavad Gita Time spoke of Itself, revealing Its might through the lips of Krishna. In fact, no other text could compare in importance with the few words spoken by Krishna while demonstrating the splendour of His form to the stunned Arjuna. The lotus-eyed, dark-skinned disturber of peace, the Avatar of Lord Vishnu, declared directly: "I am Time."

Although every living being can be regarded as an expression of Time, no other records exist of direct communication or meeting of Time with mortal beings. The limited human imagination could not have created the Bhagavad Gita. Could it be that this book was written by Time itself? Who, besides Arjuna, could have received Time's revelation; to whom, besides him, did It reveal Its true face? Only a universal consciousness is capable of giving the description of Time and space the way it is given in the 11th chapter of the Gita, "Vishvarupa Darshana Yoga" [footnote 134: Vishvarupa ("universal form") — an iconographic form and theophany of a deity. In modern Hinduism it is most often associated with Vishnu. The most famous description of the Lord's form is given in the Bhagavad Gita. Vishvarupa is considered the supreme form of the Lord — the entire universe is contained in the body of the Deity. Shailendra Sharma interprets this form of the Absolute as Time. Vishvarupa Darshana Yoga is the eleventh chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, in which Arjuna asks Krishna to reveal His universal cosmic form. Arjuna is granted divine vision and the ability to behold all creation in the body of the Supreme Lord.]. When Krishna revealed His Universal Cosmic Form, Arjuna was stunned. It seems that Time entered the body of Krishna so that such communication became possible, since to perceive and endure the true form of Time would be beyond any mortal.

In the womb of Time lived all beings and inanimate objects; the world of gross elements came into conflict with the astral planes because its existence was finite. The portrait of the Universal Being given in the Gita was staggering. For the human mind the omnipresent true form of Time was a terrifying revelation. Its countless "mouths" never ceased to "eat," "lick," "taste," and devour material worlds. And at the same time, Time went on creating new beings and entities. Being the Lord of all changes, It was constant, eternal, and continued, never coming to an end.

Re-reading these lines, he thought that the ancients had been somewhat limited in their means of expressing such concepts. Seeing how Time filled and pervaded space, the stunned Arjuna exclaimed: "Your mouths and hands are everywhere!" — and this was correct, if not detailed, description. As the son of the god Indra, Arjuna was aware of the astral side of existence, although his perception of reality remained altogether human. Therefore Arjuna could not clearly explain what stood before him. Bewildered, astonished, unable to classify what he saw, he used comparative terms accessible to mortals. By extending one's arm, one can scale that which lies within reach. Thousands of years ago an outstretched arm was a unit of measure for space. To embrace all directions at once required many limbs — and Arjuna used these descriptive epithets and metaphors to explain the indescribable. What was truly astonishing: the human mind cannot hold the splendour of Truth for very long. When in a later chapter of the Mahabharata Arjuna asks for a repetition of the teaching he had forgotten in the heat of battle, Krishna answers: "That I cannot repeat; at that time I spoke being in a yogic state…" Time had left the body of Krishna, and the lesson was over.

Having realised the nature of Time, the mind went on to recognise Its chief property: no one could escape It until Time itself permitted them to. It pervaded the Void, but what was the nature of this Void? Or did Time itself sustain Space? The Void was mentioned in yogic and Buddhist texts, but that Shunya was conscious was nowhere mentioned. This was astonishing, for Time and the Void were conscious beings. Even taken separately they are the continuation of one another, though no sharp boundary between them exists. Yet with their separation Duality begins.

...The conception of the arising of Matter came to him as a clear realisation: "From the union of Time and the Void is born conscious Matter," — and this was a fantastic revelation. He tried to explain its meaning to friends and disciples, and they were enthralled by the grandeur of these ideas. People listened with reverence, catching the essence but not fully grasping it. And yet he felt the need to set forth these deepest meanings, embedded in the very Universe, so that people could decipher them. But, as it turned out, the listeners were not entirely prepared.

Once he addressed one of the visitors: "Do you know what lies between us?" The man became alarmed: "What?" — "The Void!" After this brief dialogue the guest disappeared for a long time.

The realisation of the Gita's message took years, even after the work on the commentaries was finished. Analysing his own long-term transformations, he was firmly convinced: it was the meeting with Babaji that expanded his consciousness. The awareness of Time and the Void could happen only by the grace of the Master. Babaji's own immortality was confirmation of His ability to merge with Time, to travel within It and through It; the Master of the parampara is one with Time, and such unity could be allowed only by Time itself.

Sadhana built a bridge between the present and the future. The mortar of the past bound the supports of the present. The cement of Knowledge, of Selfhood, and of its Origin, slowly strengthened his sense of being. These realisations linked the birth of the spirit with the infinity of Time, where they were destined to meet again.

The Great Clockmaker

Once during a geometry lesson the teacher spoke of points supposedly always forming a line: a point has neither form nor volume, and yet an infinite straight line is made up of a multitude of them. He was puzzled: "How can something formless form a line?" — "Because that's how it is written in the textbook!" — the teacher was visibly irritated. He decided not to insist on an explanation, though the argument seemed weak. Without having form, something had a name, existed without coordinates of its own, and even produced another object. The manifestation of Time can be grasped only through Its effect on the material world; many forms of Matter were born, existed for some time, and then disappeared again. Traces of this process are everywhere — on man-made things, in nature, inside and outside human bodies.

People perceived the Void as infinite space filled with forms; eternity was measured as the boundless space containing temporal forms. Time pervaded Space, but to draw a sharp boundary between them was impossible, however he concentrated on this phenomenon. Time was not created by God; It Itself begot the gods and destroyed them when they ceased to be worshipped. Forgotten by men, the gods dissolved into Time.

Each of the deities performed his role. In Hinduism, Brahma is the venerated creator, Vishnu is appointed caretaker and preserver, and Shiva is the destroyer — and they represent aspects of Time, existing within It simultaneously. The birth and death of various gods are described in many texts, but the birth of Shiva is nowhere mentioned, as though He had neither beginning nor end. Probably for this reason He is worshipped as Time, taking various forms. Time is Death; and simultaneously Life; Its flow runs through everything, in everything. There are no contradictions in the nature of Time, which destroys in order to recreate.

From the moment of birth everyone moves toward death; any form is illusory, temporary, since the matter of which it is made changes constantly. The very concept of "infinity" is rather limited, defining something that lasts longer than a human life, no more. Possessing a limited and exhaustible resource of time, people are convinced that larger stretches of time imply eternity. Therefore for beings limited in time, even rational ones, it is hard to imagine anything beginningless and endless. So an intestinal bacterium might take a human body for an eternal universe.

The Bible was worth occasional paraphrasing: "From Time we came, and into Time we are destined to return…" Yet the forces existing beyond temporal limits were the very laws of being to be studied and known through the human mind. And the mind is truly beginningless and endless — only it has been stuffed into a vessel with an expiry date. And the owners of these vessels were busy with other urgent matters: securing food, reproducing, and multiplying their own vanity through the accumulation of property. Time did not interest those who were limited in time. He almost pitied the physicists trying to understand the nature of Time by observing physical objects — it is a long detour to Truth. The key to understanding the phenomenon of Time lay beyond material forms and their properties.

Time and the Void are Siamese twins, eternal lovers. Awareness of the power and immense potential of the Void could bring people closer to understanding the nature of Time — it had to take… some time.

In the preface to his commentaries on the Bhagavad Gita he wrote:
"How long can I alone call upon people to know Time? Who will listen to me? Looking at me, seemingly limited in Time, will anyone pay attention to what I say about Time?"

The nature of Time did not yield to direct cognition, but Its influence was felt continually. Time is necessary for the growth of any organism, idea, phenomenon, or understanding, and results become evident only… in time. And then everything that ripens, that is created and realised, vanishes again into It. Nor was yogic sadhana an exception. And yet its results led to the realisation of the great union of Time and the Void, of spirit and body, of Life and Death. Yoga is the compass in the world of concepts beyond the mind.

Answering the question of what exactly was happening before the creation of the world, he replied: Creation was always in the womb of Time and was born from Time. When the time of Creation runs out, it again vanishes into Time — and only Time remains. It exists outside space and is connected to nothing. Everything that related to It, that was measured by It, had its term. Eternal, indestructible, It pervaded all and was present in every one, and the human body itself is a sentence of Time, Its perishable fruit. People limited in time lived as long as their hard-drive and software allowed; physiologically, karmically, and mentally they were programmed only for a definite time, and then the warranty granted them expired. Tirelessly and methodically Time went on creating myriads of living bodies and incarnating itself in them as souls, in order to know Its own nature, to study and experience Itself. There was no other path of self-knowledge for It, for Time has no sense of time.

The life of beings and inanimate objects was a millisecond of Time; the Void was Its dial; the changes of the universal cycles were the hour-hands. The self-knowledge of the Great Clockmaker did not cease for a moment.

The Art of Dying. The Alternative Way of Escape

results.english:
To get used to repeated samadhi experiences also took time. Even the brief anxiety before "immersion" was part of the process. After several weeks the apprehensions diminished. Beginning the work on the commentaries to the Gita, he was still digesting the incredible impressions of his nightly out-of-body "sorties." The decision to describe the post-mortem experience came quite naturally and was based on the supposition that such explanations might serve as a useful guide for practitioners and seekers. They were to know exactly what happens to homeless souls.

The most vivid of the commentaries spoke of the immediate sensations of the post-mortem state: the just-released spirit is bewildered; he does not realise that the body is no longer a refuge to him, and experiences dizziness and confusion. Then, realising the obvious, he enters a state of shock. The symptoms were described so meticulously that anyone who read the commentaries attentively could understand: the author is describing personal experience. The narration was so deep and powerful because he himself had lived through these emotions. In particular, the brief panic caused by the absence of breath. And the absence of heartbeat — at which the heart still slowly and irregularly contracted, as though trying not to forget its purpose. These contractions were the sole indicator of life in the numb, motionless bodily shell. The other signs of life were gone, but the body still remained the anchor, the foothold of the spirit. He was pierced by an acute fear, yet astonishment was stronger, and probably for that reason he continued in the trance. Or in suspended animation, whatever the scientific term may be.

Fear was perfectly understandable and even appropriate: the logical mind (or what was left of it) was anxious over the possible loss of refuge. The mental imprint of death — the "extraction" of spirit from body — is linked with the most ancient form of possessiveness and the pain of separation; the loss of property is always traumatic, unless one has been taught to see the temporary nature of ownership and understands that the spirit always inhabits various "houses" and "estates."

"O Arjuna! All worlds, including the Brahma-Loka, recur; but, O Kaunteya! There is no rebirth after attaining Me." Bhagavad Gita, chapter 8, shloka 16.

Commentary: "All souls in this universe are born again and again, until they know the essence of indestructible Brahman, the consciousness of Time. Knowing this once, the soul no longer accepts birth and is not limited by a body. Moreover, especially for people who have not practised yoga and have not been able to know the dazzling radiance of Time, a description is given of certain states that follow soon after death. If, knowing of them, the ordinary person is able to maintain fearlessness at the moment of death, he too will be liberated from the bonds of birth. When the breath slows at the moment of death, together with the fear of death there arises a sense of lightness. A very strong feeling of dizziness sets in when the heart stops, and sounds like whistling, rumbling, or the ringing of little bells are heard. At this time the consciousness of the dying person leaves the body. Depending on the individual level of spirituality the dizzy sensation can last from a few moments to three or four days. In the period when consciousness leaves the physical body, the person receives a brief experience of perception of the great radiance of Time. Not having (or remembering) such an experience in the past, ordinary people are frightened. This brief phenomenon is similar to the feeling experienced by someone who has been locked in a dark room for a long time and is suddenly brought out into bright sunlight. If this does not confuse the person, and he remembers that it is this great Radiance that created his physical body, and the whole of Creation as well, then the person has a chance of merging with It. If, at the sight of this dreadful radiance, the person is frightened and tries to hide from it, the radiance will at once disappear, and the person will find himself outside the body. At this time his consciousness, having lost the support of the body, experiences a sense of emptiness. Until this moment the person does not realise that he has died. He sees his loved ones, tries to speak to them, but no one hears him. All his attempts are fruitless. Looking at the funeral ceremony and the last rites being performed over his body, he gradually begins to understand that he has already died. At this moment (sometimes earlier) he again sees the Higher Radiance. This time the Radiance resembles a pure shimmering light in the form of a huge dazzling golden circle with a centre of dark-blue colour. This is the direct vision of the consciousness of the Void. Looking at this great Light, the dead person hears the sounds of the 'anahada nada' [footnote 135: Anahada Nada, Anahad Naad — the primordial cosmic sound experienced inwardly in the higher states of meditation. It is the sound of the Void, the sound of the creation of the Universe; the sound beyond sound — the sound that gives rise to everything.] proceeding from the centre of the circle. They resemble the rumbling of thunder issuing simultaneously from a thousand clouds. If the person is not afraid and is able to keep the firm conviction: 'This is my form. It is precisely this radiance that manifested my consciousness,' — then he can merge with the Radiance and be liberated from the agonising duty of being born again. Those frightened by this experience go to heaven or hell, according to their level of consciousness. They remain bound, and these bonds compel them to be born again and again. Yogis acquire such an experience while alive, in the physical body, therefore they do not fear death. Through death the yogin merges with the Light of all light, the radiance of Time."

...Physiologically any death from natural causes proceeds by almost identical algorithms; the Creator (whoever He or She may be) treats everyone equally. Sooner or later every person must pass through death, but the reaction to this experience is strictly individual. Those who practise Kriya Yoga meet death every night through the state of breathlessness and the sense of separation from the physical form; experienced practitioners can hear the roar and rumble of the Nada — the music of liberation.

Various religions describe the afterlife differently, but all promise bliss and peace in exchange for a righteous life. What actually happens to souls relies on a quite different system of credits and points, although heaven and hell are also included in the package. The Tibetan Book of the Dead, the "Bardo Thödol," describes the post-mortem journey of consciousness as forty-nine days of wandering in various worlds before the next rebirth. The soul is eventually "drawn" into a womb according to individual karmic predispositions.

Written somewhere at the start of the New Kingdom (around 1550 BCE), the ancient Egyptian funerary text the "Book of Coming Forth into Day" is known to Western scholars as the Egyptian Book of the Dead. So are called the scattered fragments of manuscripts placed in burial chambers or sarcophagi. These records were issued by the priests and were the travel documents necessary for the journey — "visas" intended to guarantee a smooth passage and assimilation. The spells were instructions which accompanied the deceased to the gods, to a new birth.

Christianity and Islam, Judaism and the various tribal communities offer many versions of "heaven" and "hell," but all religions agree on one thing: the last rites are sacred. What is done with the body — be it cremation, burial, sky-burial, or water-burial — directs the spirit into a specific realm, to a specific Deity. The last rites determine the destination, and the subsequent transformation depends on the "customs" of the receiving egregor [footnote 136: Egregor — an esoteric concept representing a non-physical entity arising from the collective thoughts of a particular group of people. Sometimes it is regarded as the collective mind of a religious community or even a nation. The term "collective entity" is a synonym of egregor.]. But there exists also a window of opportunity, when the soul may refuse the invitation to the next cycle of rebirths and try to return to the Source. To the place from which everything came, even the Gods themselves. A bold soul can refuse the patronage of intermediaries, be they priest-intermediaries or god-intermediaries. In the short interval between the moment of physical death and the funeral, before the last rituals have been performed, the spirit can break out to the Light. In the commentaries he explained this method, giving readers a road map and marking the path to liberation.

...Nowhere was the art of dying taught better than in Kriya; this yoga trained the practitioner to meet the transition without fear, acknowledging its inevitability, and even gave a feeling of joy of merging with the greatest transforming power. A negative reaction to the transition into death — fear, protest, or anger — could affect the "quality" of the next incarnation.

Prolonged oxygen-deprivation in the practice produced the sensation of separation from the body; death seemed inevitable, and this feeling evoked the acute sense of being, of "I AM." With each passage through the gates of samadhi his apprehensions diminished, becoming a shadow of instinct, although the boundary between death and life was always near. Invisible, like a laser beam, the edge beckoned, inviting him to cross to the light and remain there, but he kept at a respectful distance. The understanding of the refined complexity of the various aspects of the metamorphoses of life was growing; the passage from body to spirit was ancient and forever new, anciently known and forgotten at the same time.

Entering the yogic trance was a return to the promised land; coming out of each samadhi was only another stop in a long journey. Like a sleepy boat at the pier, the body awaited the return of the spirit…

People are not particularly afraid of what has already been processed and registered by memory; fear is caused by the unknown. Since all memories of previous deaths are erased from the database, the spirit re-lives the horror of separation from the body anew. Quite naturally one might ask: if the soul has experienced death countless times, shouldn't the mind retain some memory of it? And overcome the "phobia of death" by making death part of the already known, even if on the subconscious level?

At some moment he understood: fear of death is a safety buffer, a control mechanism. Without it the soul would not stay in the flesh for long; it would want to leave the body and return to the astral world to keep playing in the astral jungles. Physical form fulfilled the role of an educational prop, and the earthly experience was obligatory, like the completion of an educational institution. To obtain a human body was difficult, and those to whom it was issued must use the prop with maximum benefit. And therefore the fear of death was an emergency door, a programmed safety mechanism — purely physiological, but very effective. Death leaves no memory and erases the details of every passage. Leaving the body, the soul forgets the trauma until the next journey. Time after time, the memories of death are wiped clean.

But what exactly drove souls into new bodies? Time itself evaluated individual karmas and prepared the next educational plan for souls. The new life-cycle is assigned in dependence on the person's reaction to the signs of Time, Light, and Sound, on the readiness of the soul to merge with them or to return to the embodied state. Reactions always determine fate, and for that reason his commentaries to the Gita were instructions on how to act, not to react.

The primal fear of separation from form is programmed in the consciousness identically for all; only the destinies differ. The fear of losing the body and the need at once to obtain…

life_events.event: Decides during the Gita-commentary period to record his own post-mortem experience as practical guidance for practitioners.
Recognises that every Kriya practitioner meets death nightly via breathlessness; identifies the rumble of the Anahada Nada as the music of liberation heard at death.

Composes the road-map in his commentary on Gita 8.16: the dying soul has two windows to merge with Time's Radiance — the first at the moment of breath-cessation, the second when the Nada-thunder issues from the dazzling gold-with-blue-centre disc.

Trained to die nightly through the breathless suspended-animation state — the lineage's actual death-rehearsal.

reincarnation_arcs.new_life: Re-born again and again — through 49 days of Bardo wandering in the Tibetan account, or via the egregor of the funeral rite — drawn into a womb according to karmic predispositions.
evidence: Egyptian Book of the Dead 'visas' for new birth
Souls 'eventually drawn into a womb according to individual karmic predispositions'
reincarnation_arcs.guruji_role: Sharma's Gita-commentary functions as a road-map: he names the two windows (breath-stop Radiance and golden-disc-with-blue-centre Nada vision) at which a soul can refuse rebirth and merge with the Light of Time.

reincarnation_arcs.prior_life: Has met the Radiance every night in samadhi while still in the body — has already heard the Nada and recognised 'This is my form.'
reincarnation_arcs.new_life: At physical death, merges with the Light of all light — the Radiance of Time — and is liberated from rebirth.
evidence: 'Yogis acquire such an experience while alive, in the physical body, therefore they do not fear death.'
'Through death the yogin merges with the Light of all light, the radiance of Time.'
reincarnation_arcs.guruji_role: Sharma personally embodies the yogi who already rehearses death nightly; his commentary makes the same merge available to non-yogis as an emergency protocol.

soul_liberations.soul: Any 'bold soul' in the window between physical death and funeral rites
soul_liberations.troubled_by: Bound by fear of the great Radiance and by the customs of the receiving egregor that funeral rituals would deliver them to.
soul_liberations.liberated_via: Refusal of the patronage of all intermediaries (priest or god); fearless recognition of the gold-disc-with-blue-centre Radiance as one's own form; merging with the Anahada-Nada Light of Time before the last rites are performed.
soul_liberations.place: Any place — operative between moment of physical death and funeral

soul_liberations.soul: The Kriya yogi at the moment of natural death
soul_liberations.troubled_by: Nothing — the yogi has already rehearsed the passage nightly and recognises the Nada-Radiance as his own form.
soul_liberations.liberated_via: Direct merge with the Light of all light, the radiance of Time, at the breath-stop moment.

entities.name: Shailendra Sharma
entities.description: Author of the Gita commentary on 8.16 — the road-map of the post-mortem windows of liberation.

entities.name: Babaji
entities.description: Source of the higher Kriya that enables nightly death-rehearsal.

entities.name: Bhagavad Gita 8.16
entities.description: Shloka 'All worlds recur but there is no rebirth after attaining Me' — the scriptural anchor of Sharma's liberation road-map.

entities.name: Krishna
entities.description: Speaker of 8.16; promises no rebirth after attaining Him.

entities.name: Arjuna
entities.description: Addressee of 8.16.

entities.name: Brahma-Loka
entities.description: Highest of the worlds said in 8.16 to be subject to recurrence.

entities.name: Brahman
entities.description: Indestructible Reality identified by Sharma as the consciousness of Time.

entities.name: Time
entities.description: Whose great Radiance the dying soul perceives at the breath-stop moment.

entities.name: Anahada Nada
entities.description: Primordial cosmic sound 'beyond sound', the sound of the Void's creation, the rumble heard from the centre of the golden disc at death.

entities.name: Bardo Thödol
entities.description: Describes the post-mortem journey of consciousness as 49 days of wandering.

entities.name: Egyptian Book of the Dead
aliases: Book of Coming Forth into Day
entities.description: New-Kingdom (~1550 BCE) funerary text — priestly 'visas' for the deceased's safe assimilation.

entities.name: Kriya Yoga
entities.description: The lineage practice in which death is rehearsed nightly through breathless suspended animation.

entities.name: Egregor
entities.description: Non-physical entity arising from a group's collective thoughts; the funeral-rite-receiving Deity is the soul's destination egregor.

entities.name: Yama
entities.description: Implicit Lord of Death whose realm souls re-enter unless they merge with the Radiance.

relationships.evidence_quote_en: There is no rebirth after attaining Me.
the essence of indestructible Brahman, the consciousness of Time.
the dead person hears the sounds of the 'anahada nada' proceeding from the centre of the circle.
Nowhere was the art of dying taught better than in Kriya.
The last rites determine the destination.
In the commentaries he explained this method, giving readers a road map and marking the path to liberation.
Through death the yogin merges with the Light of all light, the radiance of Time.

teachings.teaching: The dying soul receives two windows to merge with the great Radiance of Time: first at breath-cessation (sudden bright-room shock of light), second after recognising one has died (golden disc with dark-blue centre, Anahada-Nada thunder from the centre) — if the soul recognises the Radiance as its own form and is unafraid, it merges and is liberated from rebirth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary on Gita 8.16 — the operational road-map. Tibetan Book of the Dead and Egyptian Book of the Dead are partial parallels; the Kriya version is the lived one.

teachings.teaching: Kriya yogins meet death nightly through the breathless suspended-animation state; they hear the Nada in life and so do not fear the death-Radiance — that is the entire technical advantage of the practice.
teachings.lens_note: Why the lineage rehearses death and not merely meditation — the rehearsal pre-loads the recognition.

teachings.teaching: Between physical death and the funeral rites there is a window in which a bold soul can refuse the patronage of priest or god and return to the Source from which even the gods came.
teachings.lens_note: The 'alternative way of escape' — bypass the egregor by acting before the last rites lock the destination.

teachings.teaching: Fear of death is a programmed safety mechanism — without it souls would abandon the body for the astral jungles; the human body is an obligatory educational prop the soul must complete.
teachings.lens_note: The biological reason for death-amnesia: if the soul remembered the previous releases, no incarnation would last long enough to learn.

teachings.teaching: Time itself evaluates each individual's karma and assigns the next life according to the soul's reaction to the signs of Time, Light, and Sound — therefore Sharma's commentary teaches how to ACT, not how to react.
teachings.lens_note: Reaction binds, action liberates — the deepest practical instruction of the Gita commentary.
